metadata.description: Open India Emission Factors database v1.2. CC0 public domain. Covers grid electricity (national + 30 states), T&D losses, road fuels, railways, road freight by vehicle class, domestic aviation, employee commuting, stationary combustion, refrigerant GWPs, waste, crop residue burning, coal mine fugitive emissions, industrial process (cement, steel, aluminium, glass, paper, ammonia, nitric acid), agriculture, DG sets, and industrial wastewater.

CEA_V21.name: CO2 Baseline Database for the Indian Power Sector, Version 21.0
CEA_V21.publisher: Central Electricity Authority (CEA), Ministry of Power, Government of India
CEA_V21.notes: All-India national factors are CEA-published. Regional and state-level factors are computed by Batchwise (batchwise.ai) from CEA V21.0 plant-level data using CEA standard grid mapping — they are not directly published by CEA.

PPAC_RR_2025_26.notes: PPAC Ready Reckoner contains petroleum sector data (prices, production, consumption, calorific values). Fuel CO2 emission factors in this dataset are derived from IPCC 2006 guidelines using PPAC/BPCL density/calorific value references.

INDIAGHGP_2015.name: India Specific Rail Transport Emission Factors for Passenger Travel and Material Transport, Version 1.0
INDIAGHGP_2015.publisher: India GHG Program (WRI India, TERI, CII)
INDIAGHGP_2015.notes: Stakeholder consultation document. Uses Indian Railways Annual Statistical Statement data. Emission factors derived using IPCC 2006 Tier 1 methodology with CEA grid emission factor for electricity traction. Last public version available; Indian Railways has not published updated sector-level emission factors.

emission_factors.factor_name: India National Grid Emission Factor (Weighted Average incl. RE & Captive)
emission_factors.source_document: CO2 Baseline Database for the Indian Power Sector, Version 21.0
emission_factors.methodology: UNFCCC CDM Tool to Calculate the Emission Factor for an Electricity System (Version 7.0). Weighted average of CO2 emissions from all thermal power stations divided by total net electricity generation from all sources (thermal, nuclear, hydro, solar, wind, biomass). CEA-published value from V21.0 Results sheet.
emission_factors.notes: Use for location-based Scope 2 accounting under GHG Protocol. Applicable under SEBI BRSR Core and BEE CCTS frameworks. Factor includes renewable energy and captive generation in the denominator.

emission_factors.source_document: CO2 Baseline Database for the Indian Power Sector, Version 21.0
emission_factors.methodology: CDM Simple Operating Margin: emission factor of plants whose generation would be displaced by a new generator. Used in CDM project-based accounting. CEA-published value from V21.0 Results sheet.
emission_factors.notes: Primarily for CDM / carbon credit project accounting, not corporate Scope 2 inventory.

emission_factors.source_document: CO2 Baseline Database for the Indian Power Sector, Version 21.0
emission_factors.methodology: CDM Build Margin: emission factor of recently-built capacity additions to the grid. CEA-published value from V21.0 Results sheet.
emission_factors.notes: Primarily for CDM / carbon credit project accounting.

emission_factors.source_document: CO2 Baseline Database for the Indian Power Sector, Version 21.0
emission_factors.methodology: CDM Combined Margin = 75% Operating Margin + 25% Build Margin. Used in CDM project accounting for solar/wind projects. CEA-published value from V21.0 Results sheet.
emission_factors.notes: For solar/wind CDM projects, a project-specific combined margin may apply — check current CEA notifications. CEA V20.0 (FY 2023-24) values: CM for solar/wind = 0.861, CM for non-solar/wind = 0.758.

emission_factors.source_document: CO2 Baseline Database for the Indian Power Sector, Version 21.0 (plant-level data, aggregated by Batchwise)
emission_factors.methodology: Computed by Batchwise from CEA V21.0 plant-level Data sheet, aggregated to CEA standard Northern regional grid boundary. Not directly published by CEA — CEA publishes only the all-India aggregate.
emission_factors.notes: Coal-dominant (UP, Rajasthan, Haryana, Punjab) balanced by Himalayan hydro (HP, J&K, Uttarakhand). Sits above all-India average (0.7117).

emission_factors.source_document: CO2 Baseline Database for the Indian Power Sector, Version 21.0 (plant-level data, aggregated by Batchwise)
emission_factors.methodology: Computed by Batchwise from CEA V21.0 plant-level Data sheet, aggregated to CEA standard Western regional grid boundary. Not directly published by CEA.
emission_factors.notes: Second-highest of 5 regional grids. Coal dominant: Chhattisgarh (~99.7% coal, 0.9544 kgCO2/kWh) and MP (~94.6% coal) anchor the regional average above the all-India level.

emission_factors.methodology: Computed by Batchwise from CEA V21.0 plant-level Data sheet, aggregated to CEA standard Southern regional grid boundary. Not directly published by CEA.
emission_factors.notes: Mixed grid: coal thermal offset by Tamil Nadu wind and Karnataka solar additions.

emission_factors.source_document: CO2 Baseline Database for the Indian Power Sector, Version 21.0 (plant-level data, aggregated by Batchwise)
emission_factors.methodology: Computed by Batchwise from CEA V21.0 plant-level Data sheet, aggregated to CEA standard Eastern regional grid boundary. Not directly published by CEA.
emission_factors.notes: Highest emission intensity of all 5 regional grids. Most coal-intensive, concentrated in Jharkhand and West Bengal coal belt.

emission_factors.source_document: CO2 Baseline Database for the Indian Power Sector, Version 21.0 (plant-level data, aggregated by Batchwise)
emission_factors.methodology: Computed by Batchwise from CEA V21.0 plant-level Data sheet, aggregated to CEA standard North-Eastern regional grid boundary. Not directly published by CEA.
emission_factors.notes: Lowest emission intensity of all 5 grids due to hydro-dominant generation mix. Note: a value of 0.4338 is cited in some Batchwise tables — the 0.4285 figure is from the more recently reviewed Western grid comparison table (reviewed 26 May 2026). Verify against North-Eastern grid page directly.

emission_factors.factor_name: India National AT&C Loss (Aggregate Technical & Commercial)
emission_factors.source_document: Report on Performance of State Power Utilities FY 2022-23 (PFC), cited in Ministry of Power Rajya Sabha Q149, 25-Nov-2024
emission_factors.methodology: AT&C Loss = {1 - (Billing Efficiency × Collection Efficiency)} × 100. Covers both technical losses (conductor/transformer dissipation) and commercial losses (theft, metering errors, collection shortfalls). National aggregate weighted across all distribution utilities. Source: PFC Annual Discom Report.
emission_factors.notes: AT&C losses are broader than pure T&D (technical) losses. FY 2023-24 preliminary: 16.12%. Historical T&D (technical) loss: ~22.77% (FY 2014-15, CEA). Note: AT&C losses reduced from 23.70% in FY 2015-16 to 15.37% in FY 2022-23.

emission_factors.factor_name: Diesel (Motor Spirit HSD) — Road Combustion, Tank-to-Wheel
emission_factors.source_document: India Default GHG Emission Values V1.0 (SFC India, May 2025); based on IPCC 2006 Guidelines Vol.2 and BPCL density conversions
emission_factors.primary_source: IPCC 2006 Guidelines for National GHG Inventories, Volume 2: Energy, Chapter 3
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC Tier 1 default emission factors for liquid fuels. CO2e/kg = (CO2 + CH4×GWP_CH4 + N2O×GWP_N2O) × NCV / 10^6. Converted to per-litre using BPCL density (1 kg = 1.21 litres for diesel). TTW (tank-to-wheel) = direct combustion only; excludes upstream production.
emission_factors.notes: This is the TTW (combustion) factor. For WTW (well-to-wheel), apply an uplift factor of ~1.301 per SFC India (WTW/TTW ratio for diesel). India uses BS VI fuel since April 2020. Factor applies to road transport; stationary combustion may differ slightly.

emission_factors.factor_name: Petrol (Motor Spirit MS) — Road Combustion, Tank-to-Wheel
emission_factors.source_document: India Default GHG Emission Values V1.0 (SFC India, May 2025); based on IPCC 2006 Guidelines Vol.2 and BPCL density conversions
emission_factors.primary_source: IPCC 2006 Guidelines for National GHG Inventories, Volume 2: Energy
emission_factors.density_source: BPCL Fuel Conversion Factors (1 kg = 1.411 litres)
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC Tier 1 default emission factors. CO2e/kg = (69300 + 33×28 + 3.2×273) × 44.3 / 10^6 = 3.149 kgCO2e/kg. Per litre: 3.149 / 1.411 = 2.232 kgCO2e/L. Ethanol blending (currently 20% under E20 mandate) would reduce the effective emission factor proportionally.
emission_factors.notes: India's E20 ethanol blending mandate means actual emission factor may be ~5-8% lower in practice. Factor does not account for ethanol's biogenic CO2 offset. For ethanol-blended petrol, CO2 from biogenic ethanol portion is typically excluded per GHG Protocol.

emission_factors.factor_name: CNG (Compressed Natural Gas) — Road Combustion, Tank-to-Wheel
emission_factors.source_document: India Default GHG Emission Values V1.0 (SFC India, May 2025); based on IPCC 2006 Guidelines Vol.2
emission_factors.primary_source: IPCC 2006 Guidelines for National GHG Inventories, Volume 2: Energy, Table 3.3.1
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC Tier 1 default. CO2e/kg = (56100 + 92×28 + 3.0×273) × 48 / 10^6 = 2.855 kgCO2e/kg. Standard commercial unit for CNG in India is kg (sold by mass). Note: high CH4 factor for road transport reflects vehicle exhaust slip.
emission_factors.notes: India has ~8,687 CNG stations (PNGRB, Jan 2026). CNG for stationary combustion (industrial boilers) uses different emission factors — refer to IPCC 2006 stationary combustion table.

emission_factors.source_document: IPCC 2006 Guidelines for National GHG Inventories, Volume 2: Energy, Table 2.2
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC Tier 1 default emission factor for LPG (propane-butane mixture). CO2e/kg = (63100 × 47.3 + 5×28 × 47.3 + 0.1×273 × 47.3) / 10^6 = 2.983 kgCO2e/kg. India sells LPG in kg (domestic: 14.2 kg cylinders; commercial: 19 kg/47.5 kg). Standard for domestic cooking under PMUY and LPG distribution.
emission_factors.notes: LPG composition varies (propane-rich vs butane-rich blends); IPCC default covers standard commercial LPG. Per-litre conversion: ~1.52 kgCO2e/litre at density 0.51 kg/L. PPAC Ready Reckoner FY 2025-26 provides calorific value and density references for India-specific conversions.

emission_factors.factor_name: Indian Railways Passenger — Non-Suburban (Long Distance), Combined Diesel+Electric
emission_factors.scope: Scope 3 (Category 6: Business Travel / Category 7: Employee Commuting)
emission_factors.source_document: India Specific Rail Transport Emission Factors for Passenger Travel and Material Transport, Version 1.0 (India GHG Program, 2015)
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC Tier 1 Method A. Total GHG = (diesel consumed × diesel EF 2.651 kgCO2/L) + (electricity consumed × grid EF 0.82 kgCO2/kWh). Emissions allocated between non-suburban and suburban in proportion to their share of passenger-km. Non-suburban share: 86.7% of total passenger-km (952,476 million pkm in FY 2014-15). Result: 7,571.70 million kgCO2 ÷ 952,476 million pkm = 0.007948 kgCO2/pkm. India GHG Program report states 0.007837 for FY 2014-15.
emission_factors.notes: Combined diesel and electric traction factor. No separate electric-only or diesel-only published factors are available for Indian Railways. Average trip length non-suburban: ~241.5 km (FY 2014-15). For updated annual factors, re-derive using latest IR Annual Statistical Statement data. Lower than road transport (car ~0.17 kgCO2/pkm) by ~20×. Data vintage: 2014-15; use with caution for current-year reporting — IR energy mix has shifted significantly toward electric traction since (96%+ electrification by 2024).

emission_factors.factor_name: Indian Railways Passenger — Suburban (Commuter), Combined Diesel+Electric
emission_factors.scope: Scope 3 (Category 6: Business Travel / Category 7: Employee Commuting)
emission_factors.source_document: India Specific Rail Transport Emission Factors for Passenger Travel and Material Transport, Version 1.0 (India GHG Program, 2015)
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC Tier 1 Method A. Suburban share: 13.26% of total passenger-km (145,502.5 million pkm in FY 2014-15). Emissions allocated proportionally: 1,157.91 million kgCO2 ÷ 145,502.5 million pkm = 0.007958 kgCO2/pkm. India GHG Program report states 0.007976 for FY 2014-15.
emission_factors.notes: Suburban rail includes Mumbai EMU, Kolkata suburban, Chennai MRTS (except dedicated metro). Average trip length ~32.5 km. Higher per-km factor than non-suburban due to frequent stops, acceleration/deceleration losses. Excludes dedicated metro systems (DMRC, MMRC etc.). Data vintage: 2014-15.

emission_factors.scope: Scope 3 (Category 4: Upstream Transportation / Category 9: Downstream Transportation)
emission_factors.source_document: India Specific Rail Transport Emission Factors for Passenger Travel and Material Transport, Version 1.0 (India GHG Program, 2015)
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC Tier 1 Method A. Freight fuel consumption FY 2014-15: diesel 1.016992 million kL, electricity 4,728.339 million kWh. Total CO2 = (diesel × 2.651 kgCO2/L) + (electricity × 0.82 kgCO2/kWh) = 6,573.28 million kgCO2. Net tonne-km: 691,658 million. EF = 6,573.28 / 691,658 = 0.009504 kgCO2/tkm (FY 2012-13). FY 2014-15 value: 0.00996 kgCO2/tkm.
emission_factors.notes: Indian Railways is among the most emission-efficient rail freight systems globally. Compare: road truck ~0.062-0.096 kgCO2/tkm. Factor covers all freight wagon types (open, covered, flat, tanker). Major commodities: coal (44%), iron/steel, cement, food grains. Data vintage: 2014-15. Electrification has increased significantly since — electric traction share in freight was ~55% in FY 2014-15, now ~70%+, implying current factor may be lower with updated CEA grid EF applied.

emission_factors.source_document: 2006 IPCC Guidelines for National GHG Inventories, Volume 2: Energy, Table 2.2; India NCV from BEE CCTS Technical Guidelines FY 2024-25
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC Tier 1. CO2e/tonne = NCV × CO2_EF = 19.6 GJ/t × 96.1 kgCO2/GJ = 1883.6 kgCO2/t. India-specific NCV of 19.6 GJ/t adopted per BEE CCTS guidelines for domestic sub-bituminous coal. For Tier 1 stationary combustion, oxidation factor = 1.0. CH4 and N2O non-CO2 GHGs: CH4 = 10 kg/TJ, N2O = 1.5 kg/TJ (IPCC 2006 Table 2.3, boilers). Non-CO2 CO2e contribution (AR6 GWP): CH4 (GWP=27.9): 10×27.9×19.6/1000=5.47 kgCO2e/t; N2O (GWP=273): 1.5×273×19.6/1000=8.03 kgCO2e/t. Total with non-CO2: ~1897 kgCO2e/t.
emission_factors.notes: Applicable to Indian domestic coal (predominantly sub-bituminous, sourced from Jharkhand, Odisha, Chhattisgarh, West Bengal coalfields). Indian coal has high ash content (25-45%) and lower NCV than imported coal. For facility-specific reporting, derive NCV from proximate/ultimate analysis. BEE CCTS prescribed value: 1.8836 tCO2e/tonne (pre-calculated, includes oxidation factor=1.0). Value here is CO2 only; add CH4 and N2O for complete GHG inventory.

emission_factors.source_document: 2006 IPCC Guidelines for National GHG Inventories, Volume 2: Energy, Table 2.2; India NCV from BEE CCTS Technical Guidelines FY 2024-25
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC Tier 1. CO2e/tonne = 25.8 GJ/t × 94.6 kgCO2/GJ = 2440.68 kgCO2/t. India-specific NCV of 25.8 GJ/t for imported bituminous coal per BEE CCTS guidelines.
emission_factors.notes: Applies to coal imported primarily from Australia, Indonesia, South Africa. Higher NCV (25.8 vs 19.6 GJ/t) but lower per-GJ EF (94.6 vs 96.1 kgCO2/GJ) than Indian coal. Higher total per-tonne EF due to energy density. Major users: coastal power plants (Maharashtra, Gujarat, Tamil Nadu, Andhra Pradesh), steel and cement industries. BEE CCTS prescribed: 2.4407 tCO2e/tonne.

emission_factors.source_document: 2006 IPCC Guidelines for National GHG Inventories, Volume 2: Energy, Table 2.2; India NCV from BEE CCTS Technical Guidelines FY 2024-25
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC Tier 1. CO2e/tonne = 11.9 GJ/t × 101.0 kgCO2/GJ = 1201.9 kgCO2/t. India NCV 11.9 GJ/t for lignite per BEE CCTS guidelines.
emission_factors.notes: India has limited lignite reserves concentrated in Tamil Nadu (Neyveli Lignite Corporation), Gujarat, and Rajasthan. Highest per-GJ CO2 EF of coal types (101 vs 94-96 kgCO2/GJ). Lower NCV means highest per-tonne emission rate only possible if facility consumes very large quantities; more coal required per unit energy. BEE CCTS prescribed: 1.2019 tCO2e/tonne.

emission_factors.factor_name: Furnace Oil / Heavy Fuel Oil (HFO) — Stationary Combustion
emission_factors.source_document: 2006 IPCC Guidelines for National GHG Inventories, Volume 2: Energy, Table 2.2; density from PPAC / industry standard
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC Tier 1. CO2e/tonne = 40.4 GJ/t × 77.4 kgCO2/GJ = 3127.0 kgCO2/t. Per-litre: 3127.0 × 0.96 / 1000 = 3.002 kgCO2e/litre (density 0.96 kg/L). BEE CCTS prescribed: 3.127 tCO2e/tonne.
emission_factors.notes: Used in industrial boilers, captive power plants, marine vessels, cement kilns. Density range: 0.94-0.99 kg/L depending on grade (180 cSt vs 380 cSt). India imports substantial HFO for refineries and industrial use. BEE CCTS prescribed: 3.1270 tCO2e/tonne.

emission_factors.source_document: 2006 IPCC Guidelines for National GHG Inventories, Volume 2: Energy, Table 2.2; India NCV from BEE CCTS Technical Guidelines FY 2024-25
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC Tier 1. NCV = 36.0 GJ/1000 Nm3 (BEE CCTS default for India pipeline gas). CO2e/1000 Nm3 = 36.0 GJ × 56.1 kgCO2/GJ = 2019.6 kgCO2. CO2e/Nm3 = 2.0196 kgCO2. BEE CCTS prescribed: 2.0196 tCO2e/1000 Nm3 (= 2.0196 kgCO2e/Nm3).
emission_factors.notes: Nm3 = normal cubic metre at 0°C, 1 atm. Some Indian meters report in SCM (standard cubic metre at 15°C, 1 atm); SCM ≈ Nm3 × 1.055. Applies to natural gas from city gas distribution (CGD) networks (IGL, MGL, GAIL Gas), industrial pipeline gas. For road transport CNG, see record 'fuel-cng-road-ttw' (higher CH4 slip EF). BEE CCTS: 2.0196 tCO2e per 1000 Nm3 = 2.0196 kgCO2e/Nm3.

emission_factors.unit: kgCO2e/tonne (biogenic, excluded from Scope 1 per IPCC convention)
emission_factors.source_document: 2006 IPCC Guidelines for National GHG Inventories, Volume 2: Energy, Table 2.2 and Table 2.3 (Biomass/Wood waste)
emission_factors.methodology: Per IPCC convention, biogenic CO2 from biomass/bagasse combustion is excluded from Scope 1 (counted as carbon-neutral short-cycle carbon). Actual CO2 released ≈ 8.0 GJ/t × 112 kgCO2/GJ = 896 kgCO2/t biogenic. Non-CO2 GHGs (IPCC 2006 Table 2.3 for Wood/Wood Waste boilers): CH4 EF = 300 kg/TJ; N2O EF = 4.0 kg/TJ. Bagasse NCV ≈ 8.0 GJ/t (at ~50% moisture content, typical Indian sugar mills). CH4: 300 × 8.0/1000 = 2.4 kgCH4/t × GWP 27.9 = 66.96 kgCO2e/t. N2O: 4.0 × 8.0/1000 = 0.032 kgN2O/t × GWP 273 = 8.74 kgCO2e/t. Total non-CO2 CO2e: ~75.7 kgCO2e/t (reported in Scope 1).
emission_factors.notes: India is among the world's largest bagasse users for cogeneration (sugar sector). Bagasse moisture and NCV vary: NCV 7.5-9.5 GJ/t at 45-55% moisture. For dry biomass (rice husk: ~15 GJ/t; wood: ~15-19 GJ/t), recalculate using respective NCV. Biogenic CO2 must be reported separately (as a memo item) in GHG inventories under SEBI BRSR and GHG Protocol. Scope 1 inventory includes only CH4 + N2O (value_non_co2_ghg_per_tonne). BEE CCTS convention: biomass = 0.0 tCO2e/tonne (biogenic treatment). Upstream emissions from biomass supply chain are Scope 3.

emission_factors.source_document: Climate Change 2021: The Physical Science Basis (IPCC AR6 WGI), Chapter 7 Supplementary Material, Table 7.SM.7
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC AR6 GWP100. AR6 value (1960) is ~11% higher than AR5 value (1760) due to updated radiative efficiency and atmospheric chemistry. R-22 is an ozone-depleting substance phased out under Montreal Protocol; use and import restricted in India.
emission_factors.notes: R-22 phase-out completed in India for new equipment under Ozone Depleting Substances Rules. Existing equipment may still use R-22 (service permitted). Scope 1 fugitive emission = quantity of refrigerant leaked × 1960 kgCO2e/kg.

emission_factors.source_document: Climate Change 2021: The Physical Science Basis (IPCC AR6 WGI), Chapter 7 Supplementary Material, Table 7.SM.7
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC AR6 GWP100. AR6 value (771) is ~14% higher than AR5 value (675). R-32 is widely adopted in India for split air conditioners as R-22 replacement.
emission_factors.notes: Dominant refrigerant in new split AC units in India (replacing R-22 and R-410A). Mildly flammable (A2L); handle per safety protocols. Comparative GWP100: R-32 (771) < R-22 (1960) < R-410A (2256).

emission_factors.source_document: Climate Change 2021: The Physical Science Basis (IPCC AR6 WGI), Chapter 7 Supplementary Material, Table 7.SM.7
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC AR6 GWP100. AR6 value (1526) is ~17% higher than AR5 value (1300). Common in automotive AC, medium-temperature refrigeration, and chillers.
emission_factors.notes: Used in vehicle AC systems across India's automotive fleet. Subject to phase-down under Kigali Amendment to Montreal Protocol. Note: older databases may list GWP as 1,300 (AR5) or 1,430 (AR4) — use 1,526 for AR6-based reporting.

emission_factors.source_document: Climate Change 2021: The Physical Science Basis (IPCC AR6 WGI), Chapter 7 Supplementary Material, Table 7.SM.7 (component values); blend GWP mass-weighted
emission_factors.methodology: Blend GWP = mass-weighted average of component AR6 GWP100 values. = 0.44×3740 + 0.52×5810 + 0.04×1526 = 1645.6 + 3021.2 + 61.0 = 4727.8 ≈ 4728. Component GWPs from IPCC AR6 WGI Table 7.SM.7: HFC-125=3740, HFC-143a=5810, HFC-134a=1526.
emission_factors.notes: Used in commercial refrigeration (cold chains, supermarkets, food processing). Very high GWP — being phased down. AR5 value was 3922; AR6 value is 4728 (~21% higher). Note: many Indian databases still cite 3922 (AR4/AR5) — use 4728 for AR6-based reporting.

emission_factors.source_document: Climate Change 2021: The Physical Science Basis (IPCC AR6 WGI), Chapter 7 Supplementary Material, Table 7.SM.7 (component values); blend GWP mass-weighted
emission_factors.methodology: Blend GWP = 0.50×771 + 0.50×3740 = 385.5 + 1870 = 2255.5 ≈ 2256. Component GWPs from IPCC AR6 WGI Table 7.SM.7: HFC-32=771, HFC-125=3740.
emission_factors.notes: Dominant refrigerant in commercial and residential AC in India (2015-2023 era). Being replaced by R-32 in new equipment under Kigali Amendment phase-down. AR5 value was 2088; AR6 value is 2256 (~8% higher). Phase-out of new R-410A equipment manufacture underway globally.

emission_factors.source_document: Climate Change 2021: The Physical Science Basis (IPCC AR6 WGI), Chapter 7 Supplementary Material, Table 7.SM.7 (component values); blend GWP mass-weighted
emission_factors.notes: Used as R-22 drop-in replacement in older AC and refrigeration systems. Common in commercial HVAC (VRF systems, chillers). AR5 value was 1774; AR6 value is 1906 (~7.5% higher).

emission_factors.source_document: Climate Change 2021: The Physical Science Basis (IPCC AR6 WGI); IPCC 2006 Guidelines
emission_factors.methodology: NH3 has GWP100 = 0 (not a greenhouse gas; rapidly oxidized in atmosphere). No CO2e contribution from fugitive ammonia emissions.
emission_factors.notes: Zero climate impact refrigerant. Widely used in India for large industrial cold storage (food, pharma, fish). Toxic (B2L safety class) — requires engineering controls and gas detection. Indirect emissions from electricity consumed by ammonia refrigeration systems apply (Scope 2).

emission_factors.source_document: Climate Change 2021: The Physical Science Basis (IPCC AR6 WGI); by definition (CO2 reference gas)
emission_factors.methodology: CO2 is the reference gas for GWP calculations; GWP100 = 1 by definition.
emission_factors.notes: Ultra-low GWP refrigerant used in transcritical CO2 systems (commercial refrigeration, heat pumps). Growing adoption globally; limited but increasing use in India. Systems operate at high pressure (~100 bar); require specialized equipment and training.

emission_factors.factor_name: Municipal Solid Waste — Landfill CH4 Emissions (Lifetime, No Gas Recovery), India
emission_factors.unit: kgCO2e/tonne MSW (lifetime, attributional, no gas capture)
emission_factors.source_document: 2006 IPCC Guidelines for National GHG Inventories, Volume 5: Waste, Chapter 3 (First Order Decay model); India DOC from India's National GHG Inventory (MoEFCC)
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC 2006 Vol 5 Chapter 3 First Order Decay (FOD) simplified to a lifetime total factor. CH4 generated per tonne MSW = DOC × DOCf × F × (16/12) × MCF × (1-OX) = 0.12 × 0.5 × 0.5 × 1.3333 × 1.0 × 0.9 = 0.036 tCH4/tonne MSW (lifetime). CO2e = 0.036 × 27.9 × 1000 = 1004 kgCO2e/tonne. India-specific DOC = 0.12 tC/tonne: lower than IPCC default (0.17 for OECD) due to high food waste content (~50-55%) and low paper/cardboard fraction (~5-8%) in Indian MSW. DOC sources: India's Third National Communication to UNFCCC (MoEFCC, 2023).
emission_factors.notes: This is an ATTRIBUTIONAL LIFETIME factor — represents total CH4 that will be emitted over the coming ~100 years from 1 tonne of waste deposited today in an unlined/managed anaerobic landfill with no gas capture. For corporate Scope 3 waste reporting (GHG Protocol), use this factor for waste-to-landfill. For annual national inventory, apply the FOD model year-by-year (emissions in a given year come from all historical waste, not just current-year deposits — those are much lower). If gas recovery/flaring is in place, multiply by (1 - recovery_efficiency). Biogenic CO2 from waste decomposition is not included (excluded per IPCC convention). Leachate-related emissions not included. India MSW composition: ~50-55% food/organic, ~5-8% paper, ~5-7% plastic, remainder inert — resulting in lower DOC than developed nations.

emission_factors.factor_name: Domestic Wastewater — Centralized Aerobic Treatment (STP), India
emission_factors.scope: Scope 1 (direct treatment emissions) / Scope 3 Category 5 (waste generated)
emission_factors.source_document: 2006 IPCC Guidelines for National GHG Inventories, Volume 5: Waste, Chapter 6; IPCC 2019 Refinement
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC 2006 Vol 5 Chapter 6 Tier 1 approach. N2O from treatment: EF = 0.016 kg N2O-N/person/year (IPCC 2019 Refinement, Table 6.6A). Flow = 150 L/person/day = 54.75 m3/person/year. N2O = 0.016 kg N2O-N × (44/28) / 54.75 m3 = 0.000458 kgN2O/m3. N2O CO2e = 0.000458 × 273 = 0.125 kgCO2e/m3. CH4 from aerobic treatment (residual): aerobic reactors produce negligible CH4; sludge handling contributes ~0.04-0.10 kgCO2e/m3. CH4 from effluent discharge: EF = 0.048 kgCH4/kgBOD remaining in effluent. Remaining BOD ≈ 25 mg/L (after 90% removal): 0.025 × 0.048 × 27.9 = 0.033 kgCO2e/m3. Total approximate: 0.125 + 0.075 (sludge) + 0.033 = ~0.23-0.30 kgCO2e/m3. Rounded to 0.25 kgCO2e/m3 as central estimate.
emission_factors.notes: APPROXIMATE FACTOR — actual emissions vary significantly by treatment technology, plant performance, and sludge management. Range: 0.10-0.50 kgCO2e/m3. This factor covers direct (Scope 1) emissions from the treatment process. Electricity for pumping and aeration is a separate Scope 2 emission (~0.3-0.8 kWh/m3 × grid EF). For anaerobic/poorly managed STPs (common in smaller Indian towns): CH4 = 0.48 kgCH4/kgBOD removed (BOD 250 mg/L, 85% removal) = 0.48 × 0.213 = 0.102 kgCH4/m3 → 0.102 × 27.9 = 2.85 kgCO2e/m3 from CH4 alone. India STPs: As of 2021, installed capacity ~26,869 MLD against daily generation ~72,368 MLD (CPCB) — majority of wastewater is inadequately treated or untreated.

emission_factors.factor_name: Cement – Process CO2 from Clinker Production
emission_factors.source_document: IPCC 2006 Guidelines Vol 3 Ch 2 (Mineral Industry Emissions)
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC 2006 Tier 1 default: CaO fraction = 0.646 kg CaO/kg clinker; CO2/CaO = 44/56 = 0.7857; base EF = 0.507 tCO2/t clinker. CKD correction factor = 1.02 (IPCC 2006 default, Eq 2.3). Final EF = 0.507 × 1.02 = 0.5171 tCO2/t clinker.
emission_factors.notes: This is the process CO2 from carbonate decomposition (calcination) only; does not include energy-related combustion CO2 from kiln fuel. India clinker-to-cement ratio is ~72% (FY2024) per Cement Manufacturers Association (CMA), trending toward 68% by FY2028 due to blended cement uptake.

emission_factors.factor_name: Cement – Process CO2 per Tonne Cement (India Average)
emission_factors.source_document: IPCC 2006 Guidelines Vol 3 Ch 2; Cement Manufacturers Association India (CMA)
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC 2006 Tier 1 clinker EF (517.1 kgCO2/t clinker, with CKD correction) × India clinker-to-cement ratio of 0.72 (FY2024 average per CMA). EF = 517.1 × 0.72 = 372.3 kgCO2/t cement.
emission_factors.notes: Clinker-to-cement ratio varies by plant and product mix. India sector average is ~72% (2024); declining as blended cement (PPC, PSC) share rises. Use 68% ratio for FY2028+ projections. Does not include fuel combustion CO2.

emission_factors.factor_name: Steel – Integrated Blast Furnace / Basic Oxygen Furnace (BF-BOF) Route
emission_factors.source_document: IPCC 2006 Guidelines Vol 3 Ch 4; MoEFCC Third National Communication 2023
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC 2006 Tier 1 default for integrated BF-BOF route: 1.46 tCO2/t crude steel. Includes CO2 from coke, sinter plant, blast furnace, basic oxygen furnace, and auxiliary energy use. Consistent with BEE PAT sector benchmarks and MoEFCC Third NC (2023) estimates for India's primary steel sector. India BF-BOF route accounts for ~50% of crude steel production.
emission_factors.notes: BF-BOF is the dominant route for primary (virgin iron ore-based) steel. Best-in-class India plants achieve ~1.8-2.0 tCO2/t steel (including indirect), with direct process at ~1.46. Excludes scope 2 electricity.

emission_factors.factor_name: Steel – Electric Arc Furnace / Direct Reduced Iron (EAF-DRI) Route
emission_factors.source_document: IPCC 2006 Guidelines Vol 3 Ch 4; MoEFCC Third National Communication 2023
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC 2006 Tier 1 default for EAF with DRI (gas-based): ~0.80 tCO2/t crude steel. Includes CO2 from natural gas reduction in DRI shaft furnaces and auxiliary fuel use. India is the world's largest DRI producer; ~50% of crude steel is via EAF-DRI route. Excludes electricity-related Scope 2 emissions from EAF arc.
emission_factors.notes: EAF-DRI with scrap mix may have lower effective EF depending on scrap fraction. Coal-based DRI (rotary kiln) used in some India plants has higher EF (~1.2 tCO2/t). This factor is for gas-based DRI + EAF (Scope 1 direct only). Add Scope 2 grid electricity EF × ~400 kWh/t steel for total.

emission_factors.factor_name: Aluminium – Primary Smelting, Soderberg Technology (Process CO2 + PFC)
emission_factors.source_document: IPCC 2006 Guidelines Vol 3 Ch 4
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC 2006 Tier 1 defaults for Soderberg technology. Anode CO2 (carbon consumption): 1.65 tCO2/t Al = 1650 kgCO2. PFC CF4: 0.8 kg/t Al × GWP 7380 = 5904 kgCO2e. PFC C2F6: 0.085 kg/t Al × GWP 12400 = 1054 kgCO2e. Total = 8608 kgCO2e/t Al. GWP values from IPCC AR6 WGI Table 7.SM.7 (100-year). Excludes smelter electricity (Scope 2); typical smelter uses ~13,500 kWh/t Al.
emission_factors.notes: Soderberg technology (continuous Soderberg anodes) was historically dominant in India (NALCO Damanjodi, Vedanta Jharsuguda older lines). Newer plants use pre-bake technology with significantly lower PFC: CF4 ~0.1 kg/t, C2F6 ~0.01 kg/t, giving ~1800 kgCO2e/t Al. Use pre-bake factor for modern Indian smelters (post-2010). This Soderberg factor represents older plant profile. PFC emissions are highly variable and technology/management dependent.

emission_factors.factor_name: Glass – Container Glass (Process CO2 from Carbonate Decomposition)
emission_factors.source_document: IPCC 2006 Guidelines Vol 3 Ch 2 (Mineral Industry Emissions)
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC 2006 Tier 1 default for container glass (bottles, jars). Process CO2 from decomposition of carbonates (limestone, soda ash, dolomite) in raw batch. Typical batch carbonate content yields ~0.21 tCO2/t glass melt. Cullet (recycled glass) fraction reduces process CO2 proportionally (no carbonate decomposition from cullet).
emission_factors.notes: Process CO2 only; excludes fuel combustion CO2 from furnace (~200-300 kgCO2/t glass additional). Cullet use in India averages ~25-30%; each 10% additional cullet reduces batch CO2 by ~10%. Flat glass has slightly different batch composition – see separate record.

emission_factors.factor_name: Glass – Flat Glass (Process CO2 from Carbonate Decomposition)
emission_factors.source_document: IPCC 2006 Guidelines Vol 3 Ch 2 (Mineral Industry Emissions)
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC 2006 Tier 1 default for flat glass (float glass process). Slightly lower process CO2 than container glass due to different batch composition (lower soda ash fraction, higher silica sand). Base factor ~0.20 tCO2/t flat glass.
emission_factors.notes: Process CO2 from carbonate decomposition only; excludes natural gas furnace combustion CO2 (typically ~200 kgCO2/t for float glass furnaces). India flat glass market dominated by Guardian, Saint-Gobain, Hindusthan National Glass.

emission_factors.source_document: IPCC 2006 Guidelines Vol 4 Ch 10 (Table 10.11); MoEFCC Third National Communication 2023
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC 2006 Vol 4 Ch 10 Tier 1 default for South Asia: 68 kgCH4/head/year. Converted to CO2e using IPCC AR6 GWP100: CH4 = 27.9. CO2e = 68 × 27.9 = 1897.2 kgCO2e/head/year. Consistent with India's national GHG inventory methodology as reported in Third NC 2023.
emission_factors.notes: Includes crossbred and exotic dairy breeds. India average body weight ~250 kg for crossbred, ~400 kg for exotic. Emissions higher in improved productivity herds.

emission_factors.source_document: IPCC 2006 Guidelines Vol 4 Ch 10 (Table 10.11); MoEFCC Third National Communication 2023
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC 2006 Vol 4 Ch 10 Tier 1 default for South Asia: 47 kgCH4/head/year. Converted to CO2e using IPCC AR6 GWP100: CH4 = 27.9. CO2e = 47 × 27.9 = 1311.3 kgCO2e/head/year. Consistent with India's national GHG inventory methodology as reported in Third NC 2023.
emission_factors.notes: Includes indigenous draught cattle, male cattle, dry cows. India indigenous cattle (zebu) have lower EF than South Asian average due to smaller body size.

emission_factors.source_document: IPCC 2006 Guidelines Vol 4 Ch 10 (Table 10.11); MoEFCC Third National Communication 2023
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC 2006 Vol 4 Ch 10 Tier 1 default for South Asia: 55 kgCH4/head/year. Converted to CO2e using IPCC AR6 GWP100: CH4 = 27.9. CO2e = 55 × 27.9 = 1534.5 kgCO2e/head/year. Consistent with India's national GHG inventory methodology as reported in Third NC 2023.
emission_factors.notes: Murrah and other dairy buffalo breeds. Buffalo account for ~42% of India's bovine enteric fermentation emissions. EF consistent with India's BUR3 inventory.

emission_factors.source_document: IPCC 2006 Guidelines Vol 4 Ch 10 (Table 10.11); MoEFCC Third National Communication 2023
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC 2006 Vol 4 Ch 10 Tier 1 default for South Asia: 5 kgCH4/head/year. Converted to CO2e using IPCC AR6 GWP100: CH4 = 27.9. CO2e = 5 × 27.9 = 139.5 kgCO2e/head/year. Consistent with India's national GHG inventory methodology as reported in Third NC 2023.
emission_factors.notes: India sheep average; largely hair sheep breeds. Rajasthan, AP, Karnataka are major sheep states.

emission_factors.source_document: IPCC 2006 Guidelines Vol 4 Ch 10 (Table 10.11); MoEFCC Third National Communication 2023
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC 2006 Vol 4 Ch 10 Tier 1 default for South Asia: 5 kgCH4/head/year. Converted to CO2e using IPCC AR6 GWP100: CH4 = 27.9. CO2e = 5 × 27.9 = 139.5 kgCO2e/head/year. Consistent with India's national GHG inventory methodology as reported in Third NC 2023.
emission_factors.notes: India goat average; mostly non-dairy meat breeds. India has ~150 million goats (2019 census).

emission_factors.factor_name: Agriculture – Manure Management – Cattle (Dairy and Non-Dairy) – Methane EF (India)
emission_factors.source_document: IPCC 2006 Guidelines Vol 4 Ch 10 (Table 10.14); MoEFCC Third National Communication 2023
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC 2006 Vol 4 Ch 10 Tier 1 default for South Asia: 1.0 kgCH4/head/year from manure management. To convert: multiply by GWP_CH4 = 27.9 (IPCC AR6) = 27.9 kgCO2e/head/year (CH4 component only). Add N2O component separately (see notes for guidance).
emission_factors.notes: Includes cattle manure in drylot (most common in India), liquid slurry (biogas digesters), and pasture. India manure management is predominantly drylot/dry storage. N2O from manure management: apply IPCC EF3 = 0.01 kgN2O-N/kgN-excreted for drylot × N excretion ~45 kgN/head/yr = ~0.71 kgN2O/head/yr = 194 kgCO2e/head/yr (N2O only, AR6 GWP).

emission_factors.factor_name: Agriculture – Manure Management – Buffalo – Methane EF (India)
emission_factors.source_document: IPCC 2006 Guidelines Vol 4 Ch 10 (Table 10.14); MoEFCC Third National Communication 2023
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC 2006 Vol 4 Ch 10 Tier 1 default for South Asia: 2.0 kgCH4/head/year from manure management. To convert: multiply by GWP_CH4 = 27.9 (IPCC AR6) = 55.8 kgCO2e/head/year (CH4 component only). Add N2O component separately (see notes for guidance).
emission_factors.notes: Buffalo manure CH4 higher than cattle due to different diet digestibility and manure VS content. IPCC 2006 Table 10.14 South Asia default. N2O from manure: ~0.63 kgN2O/head/yr = 172 kgCO2e/head/yr (using EF3 = 0.01, N excretion ~40 kgN/head/yr, IPCC AR6 GWP N2O=273).

emission_factors.factor_name: Agriculture – Manure Management – Poultry (Layer Hens and Broilers) – Methane EF (India)
emission_factors.source_document: IPCC 2006 Guidelines Vol 4 Ch 10 (Table 10.14); MoEFCC Third National Communication 2023
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC 2006 Vol 4 Ch 10 Tier 1 default for South Asia: 0.02 kgCH4/head/year from manure management. To convert: multiply by GWP_CH4 = 27.9 (IPCC AR6) = 0.56 kgCO2e/head/year (CH4 component only). Add N2O component separately (see notes for guidance).
emission_factors.notes: IPCC 2006 Table 10.14 default for poultry in warm climate. Primarily dry poultry litter system. N2O from manure: ~0.025 kgN2O/head/yr = 6.9 kgCO2e/head/yr (EF3 = 0.02 for poultry litter, N excretion ~0.8 kgN/head/yr, IPCC AR6 GWP).

emission_factors.factor_name: Agriculture – Rice Cultivation – Lowland Paddy, Kharif Season (India Average)
emission_factors.source_document: IPCC 2006 Guidelines Vol 4 Ch 5; MoEFCC Third National Communication 2023; ICAR
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC 2006 Vol 4 Ch 5 Tier 1 approach: EFc = 1.30 kgCH4/ha/day (continuously flooded, no organic amendments). Season length = 90 days (kharif). Scaling factors: SFw = 1.0 (continuously flooded), SFp = 1.0 (non-puddled baseline), SFo = 1.0 (no organic amendments). Seasonal CH4 = 1.30 × 90 = 117 kgCH4/ha/season (base). India national average adjusted to 113.0 kgCH4/ha/season based on aggregate inventory (total CH4 ~4.75 Tg/yr from ~46 Mha rice, MoEFCC TNC 2023). CO2e = 113.0 × 27.9 = 3152.7 kgCO2e/ha/season.
emission_factors.notes: India average across flooded/intermittently flooded and aerobic rice systems. Continuously flooded rice emits more CH4; System of Rice Intensification (SRI) and alternate wetting and drying (AWD) can reduce CH4 by 30-50%. Organic amendments (FYM, compost) increase CH4; adjust using IPCC SFo factors. N2O from rice soils is typically small but non-zero; use IPCC EF1 for fertilized plots.

emission_factors.factor_name: Agriculture – Direct N2O Emissions from Synthetic Nitrogen Fertilizer Application
emission_factors.source_document: IPCC 2006 Guidelines Vol 4 Ch 11 (Table 11.1)
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC 2006 Vol 4 Ch 11 Tier 1: EF1 = 0.01 kgN2O-N per kgN applied (direct soil emissions). N2O per kgN = 0.01 × (44/28) = 0.01571 kgN2O/kgN. CO2e = 0.01571 × 273 (IPCC AR6) = 4.289 kgCO2e/kgN. For urea specifically (46% N by weight): 4.289 × 0.46 = 1.973 kgCO2e/kg urea. This covers direct soil N2O only; indirect N2O (leaching, volatilisation) adds ~30% more.
emission_factors.notes: Urea application factor: 1.973 kgCO2e/kg urea (46% N). For other fertilizers: multiply kgCO2e/kgN by the N content fraction (e.g., DAP = 18% N → 0.18 × {co2e_per_kgN}; ammonium nitrate = 34% N → 0.34 × {co2e_per_kgN}). India uses ~14 million tonnes of urea annually (major source of agricultural N2O). Indirect N2O (from NH3 volatilisation and NO3 leaching) should be added using IPCC EF4 and EF5.

emission_factors.factor_name: Diesel Generator Set (<15 kVA) – CO2e per kWh Generated
emission_factors.source_document: CPCB DG Set Emission Standards (CPCB IV+); IPCC 2006 Vol 2 Ch 3
emission_factors.methodology: Derived from diesel combustion CO2e factor × fuel consumption rate. Diesel EF: 2.675 kgCO2e/L (IPCC 2006 Tier 1, see record mobile-road-diesel). Fuel consumption rate for <15 kVA: 0.38 L/kWh (industry benchmark at 75-100% rated load). EF = 0.38 × 2.675 = 1.016 kgCO2e/kWh. CPCB IV+ specifies NOx/PM/CO/HC limits only; CO2 derived from diesel combustion stoichiometry.
emission_factors.notes: Fuel consumption rate of 0.38 L/kWh is an industry benchmark for <15 kVA sets at 75%+ load. Actual EF varies with load factor, engine age, maintenance, and ambient conditions. Per-litre EF is constant at 2.675 kgCO2e/L diesel regardless of DG size. Small DG sets common in shops, homes, small offices. Lower efficiency at partial loads. Typical fuel consumption 0.35-0.42 L/kWh at 75% load.

emission_factors.factor_name: Diesel Generator Set (15–75 kVA) – CO2e per kWh Generated
emission_factors.source_document: CPCB DG Set Emission Standards (CPCB IV+); IPCC 2006 Vol 2 Ch 3
emission_factors.methodology: Derived from diesel combustion CO2e factor × fuel consumption rate. Diesel EF: 2.675 kgCO2e/L (IPCC 2006 Tier 1, see record mobile-road-diesel). Fuel consumption rate for 15–75 kVA: 0.32 L/kWh (industry benchmark at 75-100% rated load). EF = 0.32 × 2.675 = 0.856 kgCO2e/kWh. CPCB IV+ specifies NOx/PM/CO/HC limits only; CO2 derived from diesel combustion stoichiometry.
emission_factors.notes: Fuel consumption rate of 0.32 L/kWh is an industry benchmark for 15–75 kVA sets at 75%+ load. Actual EF varies with load factor, engine age, maintenance, and ambient conditions. Per-litre EF is constant at 2.675 kgCO2e/L diesel regardless of DG size. Medium small DG sets for SMEs, small commercial buildings. Typical consumption 0.30-0.35 L/kWh. CPCB IV+ norms apply from July 2019.

emission_factors.factor_name: Diesel Generator Set (75–500 kVA) – CO2e per kWh Generated
emission_factors.source_document: CPCB DG Set Emission Standards (CPCB IV+); IPCC 2006 Vol 2 Ch 3
emission_factors.methodology: Derived from diesel combustion CO2e factor × fuel consumption rate. Diesel EF: 2.675 kgCO2e/L (IPCC 2006 Tier 1, see record mobile-road-diesel). Fuel consumption rate for 75–500 kVA: 0.28 L/kWh (industry benchmark at 75-100% rated load). EF = 0.28 × 2.675 = 0.749 kgCO2e/kWh. CPCB IV+ specifies NOx/PM/CO/HC limits only; CO2 derived from diesel combustion stoichiometry.
emission_factors.notes: Fuel consumption rate of 0.28 L/kWh is an industry benchmark for 75–500 kVA sets at 75%+ load. Actual EF varies with load factor, engine age, maintenance, and ambient conditions. Per-litre EF is constant at 2.675 kgCO2e/L diesel regardless of DG size. Commercial and industrial DG sets. Most common size for offices, hospitals, factories. Typical consumption 0.26-0.30 L/kWh at full load. Best practice tier: 0.24-0.26 L/kWh.

emission_factors.factor_name: Diesel Generator Set (>500 kVA) – CO2e per kWh Generated
emission_factors.source_document: CPCB DG Set Emission Standards (CPCB IV+); IPCC 2006 Vol 2 Ch 3
emission_factors.methodology: Derived from diesel combustion CO2e factor × fuel consumption rate. Diesel EF: 2.675 kgCO2e/L (IPCC 2006 Tier 1, see record mobile-road-diesel). Fuel consumption rate for >500 kVA: 0.25 L/kWh (industry benchmark at 75-100% rated load). EF = 0.25 × 2.675 = 0.669 kgCO2e/kWh. CPCB IV+ specifies NOx/PM/CO/HC limits only; CO2 derived from diesel combustion stoichiometry.
emission_factors.notes: Fuel consumption rate of 0.25 L/kWh is an industry benchmark for >500 kVA sets at 75%+ load. Actual EF varies with load factor, engine age, maintenance, and ambient conditions. Per-litre EF is constant at 2.675 kgCO2e/L diesel regardless of DG size. Large industrial DG sets, data centres, large manufacturing. Highest efficiency class. Typical consumption 0.23-0.27 L/kWh. Often run at high load factors which improves efficiency further.

emission_factors.factor_name: Road Freight – Light Commercial Vehicle (LCV, GVW <3.5t) – Diesel WTW
emission_factors.source_document: India Default GHG Emission Values V1.0, Smart Freight Centre India & IIM Bangalore (May 2025)
emission_factors.methodology: SFC India V1.0 Table: Small Commercial Vehicles (GVW <3.5 MT, diesel, average load). WTT emission intensity: 0.0879 kgCO2e/tkm; TTW: 0.2916 kgCO2e/tkm; WTW total: 0.3795 kgCO2e/tkm. Empty factor 10.9%, average utilisation 83.0%, fuel efficiency 0.0841 L/km (laden). Distance calculated using Google Distance Matrix API + 5% DAF uplift for deviation. WTW = TTW × (1/TTW-to-WTW ratio of 0.77) using GLEC Framework v3.1 WTT uplift. Methodology compliant with ISO 14083 and GLEC Framework v3.1.
emission_factors.notes: LCV class includes 3-wheeled cargo vehicles, mini trucks, and vans common in India urban last-mile delivery. Higher per-tkm EF reflects lower payload utilisation and less efficient smaller engines. Petrol LCV WTW: 0.4124 kgCO2e/tkm; CNG LCV WTW: 0.4920 kgCO2e/tkm (from SFC India V1.0).

emission_factors.factor_name: Road Freight – Medium Commercial Vehicle (MCV, GVW 5–12t) – Diesel WTW
emission_factors.source_document: India Default GHG Emission Values V1.0, Smart Freight Centre India & IIM Bangalore (May 2025)
emission_factors.methodology: SFC India V1.0 Table: Medium Commercial Vehicles-2 (GVW 5–12 MT, diesel, average load). WTT: 0.0324 kgCO2e/tkm; TTW: 0.1075 kgCO2e/tkm; WTW total: 0.1400 kgCO2e/tkm. Empty factor 10.8%, average utilisation 78.8%, fuel efficiency 0.1494 L/km. Methodology: ISO 14083 and GLEC Framework v3.1.
emission_factors.notes: User request range is 3.5–12t GVW (spans MCV-1 and MCV-2 in SFC classification). This record uses MCV-2 (GVW 5–12t, payload 3.5–8t) as representative of the broader class. For lighter MCV (GVW 3–5t, payload 2–3.5t), SFC India V1.0 gives WTW 0.2222 kgCO2e/tkm. CNG MCV-2 alternative: WTW 0.1689 kgCO2e/tkm (SFC India V1.0).

emission_factors.factor_name: Road Freight – Heavy Commercial Vehicle (HCV, GVW >12t) – Diesel WTW
emission_factors.source_document: India Default GHG Emission Values V1.0, Smart Freight Centre India & IIM Bangalore (May 2025)
emission_factors.methodology: SFC India V1.0 Table: Heavy Commercial Vehicles-2 (GVW 20–30 MT, diesel, average load). WTT: 0.0191 kgCO2e/tkm; TTW: 0.0632 kgCO2e/tkm; WTW total: 0.0823 kgCO2e/tkm. Empty factor 12%, average utilisation 69.4%, fuel efficiency 0.2823 L/km. Methodology: ISO 14083 and GLEC Framework v3.1.
emission_factors.notes: SFC India V1.0 covers three HCV sub-classes: HCV-1 (12–20t GVW): 0.0902 kgCO2e/tkm; HCV-2 (20–30t GVW, most common class): 0.0823 kgCO2e/tkm; HCV-3 (30–50t GVW): 0.0663 kgCO2e/tkm; Tractor-trailer (30–60t GVW): 0.0551 kgCO2e/tkm. HCV-2 is chosen as representative; India national highway freight predominantly 20–30t trucks. All values are WTW (well-to-wheel) including fuel production emissions.

emission_factors.factor_name: Domestic Aviation – India All Routes – Economy Class (CO2 only)
emission_factors.source_document: ICAO Carbon Emissions Calculator Methodology; DGCA Annual Statistics 2024; IPCC 2006 Vol 2 Ch 3
emission_factors.methodology: Derived using ICAO Carbon Emissions Calculator methodology. Narrow-body fuel burn factor: ~3.91 kg fuel per 100 seat-km (A320-family, fleet average). India domestic load factor (2024): 85% per DGCA statistics (India leads global load factor rankings). Fuel per pkm = 3.91/0.85 = 4.60 kg/100 pkm = 0.0460 kg/pkm. Kerosene CO2 EF = 3.16 kgCO2/kg (ICAO/IPCC 2006 Vol 2 Ch 3). CO2 = 0.0460 × 3.16 = 0.145 kgCO2/pkm (all domestic routes, fleet average). Economy class seat allocation factor = 1.0 (no cabin class adjustment for domestic, all-economy typical). CO2 only; does not include non-CO2 radiative forcing (RF) effects.
emission_factors.notes: CO2-only factor; excludes non-CO2 climate effects (NOx-induced ozone, water vapour, contrails). GHG Protocol Scope 3 Technical Guidance recommends CO2 only for Scope 3 Category 6 reporting. For climate-impact accounting including RF effects, multiply by 1.9 (DEFRA approach) = ~0.275 kgCO2e/pkm. India domestic fleet ~75% A320-family, 15% B737-family, 10% ATR. India domestic passengers 2024: ~160 million (DGCA). Short-haul routes (<1000km) have higher per-pkm EF — see separate record.

emission_factors.factor_name: Domestic Aviation – India Short-Haul (<1000km) – Economy Class (CO2 only)
emission_factors.source_document: ICAO Carbon Emissions Calculator Methodology; DGCA Annual Statistics 2024; IPCC 2006 Vol 2 Ch 3
emission_factors.methodology: Short-haul (<1000km) EF derived from India domestic average with LTO correction. LTO (landing/takeoff) cycle accounts for ~25% of total fuel on routes <1000km vs ~15% on longer domestic routes. Base EF 0.145 kgCO2/pkm × 1.25 = 0.182 kgCO2/pkm. ICAO Carbon Emissions Calculator applies route-distance corrections using actual great-circle distances plus detour factor. Kerosene CO2 EF = 3.16 kgCO2/kg (IPCC 2006 Vol 2 Ch 3). India 85% load factor applied.
emission_factors.notes: Applies to short-haul domestic routes: Delhi-Lucknow (509km), Mumbai-Pune (149km), Bangalore-Chennai (290km), etc. Routes <500km are dominated by LTO fuel; EF could be up to 0.25 kgCO2/pkm. For trips <500km, road/rail alternatives have significantly lower emissions. CO2 only; exclude radiative forcing effects.

emission_factors.factor_name: Employee Commuting – Private Car (Petrol) – India Average
emission_factors.source_document: IPCC 2006 Vol 2 Ch 3; SFC India V1.0 (2025); MoRTH Road Transport Yearbook 2022-23
emission_factors.methodology: Derived from India average petrol car fuel consumption and occupancy. Fuel consumption: 7.5 L/100km (India urban driving average, mix of small hatchbacks and sedans; IPCC 2006 Vol 2 Ch 3 + MoRTH VAHAN fleet data). Petrol CO2e EF: 2.296 kgCO2e/L (TTW, IPCC 2006 Tier 1; see record mobile-road-petrol). CO2e per vehicle-km: 0.075 × 2.296 = 0.172 kgCO2e/km. India commute car occupancy: 1.5 persons (driver + 0.5 average passenger, MoRTH data). EF per pkm = 0.172/1.5 = 0.115 kgCO2e/pkm.
emission_factors.notes: India vehicle fleet dominated by small petrol cars (Maruti Suzuki, Hyundai i10/i20, etc.). Occupancy 1.5 is India average for private car commuting (MoRTH 2023). Urban congestion increases fuel consumption vs highway; 7.5 L/100km reflects mixed driving. For carpooling (occupancy 3.0): EF halves to ~0.057 kgCO2e/pkm. WTW (including fuel production) would add ~30%: ~0.150 kgCO2e/pkm.

emission_factors.factor_name: Employee Commuting – Private Car (Diesel) – India Average
emission_factors.source_document: IPCC 2006 Vol 2 Ch 3; SFC India V1.0 (2025); MoRTH Road Transport Yearbook 2022-23
emission_factors.methodology: Fuel consumption: 5.5 L/100km (India average for diesel sedans and SUVs; IPCC 2006 Vol 2 Ch 3 defaults + India fleet data). Diesel CO2e EF: 2.675 kgCO2e/L (TTW, IPCC 2006 Tier 1; see record mobile-road-diesel). CO2e per vehicle-km: 0.055 × 2.675 = 0.147 kgCO2e/km. Occupancy 1.5 persons (MoRTH data). EF = 0.147/1.5 = 0.098 kgCO2e/pkm.
emission_factors.notes: Diesel cars in India are predominantly mid-size sedans and SUVs (e.g., Maruti Swift Dzire, Hyundai Verna diesel, Mahindra XUV 300 diesel). Diesel share in new car sales has declined from ~50% (2012) to <20% (2024) due to BSVI norms and petrol price parity. Diesel cars achieve better fuel economy than petrol but higher NOx/PM emissions.

emission_factors.source_document: IPCC 2006 Vol 2 Ch 3; SFC India V1.0 (2025); MoRTH Road Transport Yearbook 2022-23
emission_factors.methodology: Two-wheelers are India's dominant personal transport mode. Fuel consumption: 45 km/L average (India typical: scooters ~40 km/L, motorcycles ~50 km/L; IPCC 2006 Vol 2 Ch 3 + MoRTH fleet survey). Petrol EF: 2.296 kgCO2e/L (TTW; see record mobile-road-petrol). CO2e per vehicle-km: 2.296/45 = 0.051 kgCO2e/km. Occupancy: 1.2 (most commuters ride solo; some pillion, average 1.2 per MoRTH). EF = 0.051/1.2 = 0.042 kgCO2e/pkm.
emission_factors.notes: Two-wheelers account for ~75% of registered motor vehicles in India (MoRTH 2023). Despite low per-pkm EF vs cars, their massive numbers make them significant nationally. Electric two-wheelers (Ola Electric, Ather, TVS iQube) are displacing petrol; EV two-wheeler grid EF ≈ 0.716 kgCO2/kWh × 0.03 kWh/km / 1.2 ≈ 0.018 kgCO2e/pkm (using national grid EF; much lower with renewable charging).

emission_factors.factor_name: Employee Commuting – City Bus (Diesel) – India Urban Average
emission_factors.source_document: IPCC 2006 Vol 2 Ch 3; SFC India V1.0 (2025); MoRTH Road Transport Yearbook 2022-23
emission_factors.methodology: City bus diesel fuel consumption: ~4.5 km/L (urban stop-start driving, air-conditioned buses; IPCC 2006 Vol 2 Ch 3 + MoRTH data for city bus fleet). Diesel EF: 2.675 kgCO2e/L. Per bus-km: 2.675/4.5 = 0.594 kgCO2e/km. Average bus occupancy India: ~40 passengers across the operating day (peak 70-80, off-peak 15-20; weighted average ~40 per MoRTH). EF per pkm = 0.594/40 = 0.015 kgCO2e/pkm.
emission_factors.notes: City bus is most efficient per-pkm land transport mode in India. At full capacity (80 passengers): EF drops to 0.007 kgCO2e/pkm. Electric buses (BEST Mumbai, DTC Delhi, BMTC Bangalore): EF ≈ 0.716 kgCO2/kWh × 1.2 kWh/km / 40 = 0.021 kgCO2e/pkm (grid) or nearly zero with renewable electricity. CNG buses: 0.013 kgCO2e/pkm (similar to diesel but slightly cleaner on other pollutants).

emission_factors.factor_name: Employee Commuting – Metro Rail – India Average (National Grid EF)
emission_factors.source_document: IPCC 2006 Vol 2 Ch 3; SFC India V1.0 (2025); MoRTH Road Transport Yearbook 2022-23
emission_factors.methodology: Metro energy intensity: ~0.05 kWh per passenger-km (India average, weighted across Delhi, Mumbai, Bangalore, Chennai, Hyderabad, Kolkata metro systems). Derived from: train energy consumption ~50 kWh/train-km (6-car train); average ridership ~600-700 passengers per train across operating day; energy/pkm = 50/650 = 0.077 kWh/pkm at 40% average load; adjusted to 0.05 kWh/pkm using published DMRC (Delhi Metro) benchmarks. Grid EF: 0.716 kgCO2/kWh (CEA CO2 Baseline Database V21.0, FY2024-25; see record grid-national-india). CO2e = 0.05 × 0.716 = 0.036 kgCO2e/pkm.
emission_factors.notes: Metro EF will decrease as grid decarbonises. At 0.3 kgCO2/kWh grid: EF = 0.015 kgCO2e/pkm. Delhi Metro sources ~65% from renewable energy (DMRC 2024 sustainability report). For site-specific calculation: multiply metro system kWh/pkm by applicable state grid EF (see state grid records in this database). This record uses Scope 2 location-based approach (GHG Protocol).

emission_factors.source_document: IPCC 2006 Vol 2 Ch 3; SFC India V1.0 (2025); MoRTH Road Transport Yearbook 2022-23
emission_factors.methodology: CNG auto-rickshaw fuel consumption: 28 km/kg CNG (India average; typical range 25-32 km/kg depending on load, traffic, engine condition). CNG CO2e EF (mobile, WTW): 2.855 kgCO2e/kg (from SFC India V1.0; includes CH4 and N2O at IPCC AR6 GWP100). Per vehicle-km: 2.855/28 = 0.102 kgCO2e/km. Average occupancy: 2.0 (auto-rickshaws seat 3 passengers; average 1.5-2.5 per trip, typically 2.0 for shared routes; MoRTH data). EF = 0.102/2.0 = 0.051 kgCO2e/pkm.
emission_factors.notes: CNG auto-rickshaws are a primary urban transport mode across India; Delhi has ~100,000 CNG autos. Almost entirely CNG since Supreme Court mandated CNG conversion in Delhi (2001); Mumbai, Kolkata, Chennai followed. For solo rider (occupancy 1): EF doubles to 0.102 kgCO2e/pkm. For shared auto-rickshaw (occupancy 3): EF = 0.034 kgCO2e/pkm. Electric auto-rickshaws (e-rickshaws): EF ≈ 0.019 kgCO2e/pkm (grid) — significantly lower, increasingly common.

emission_factors.factor_name: Crop Residue Burning – Rice Straw – India
emission_factors.source_document: IPCC 2006 Vol 4 Ch 2 (Table 2.5); MoEFCC Third National Communication 2023; ICAR
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC 2006 Vol 4 Ch 2 Tier 1 EFs for rice straw burning (Table 2.5). CH4 EF: 2.7 g/kg DM burned = 2.7 kgCH4/t DM; CO2e = 2.7 × 27.9 = 75.33 kgCO2e/t DM. N2O EF: 0.07 g/kg DM burned = 0.07 kgN2O/t DM; CO2e = 0.07 × 273 = 19.11 kgCO2e/t DM. Total (CH4 + N2O): 94.4 kgCO2e/t DM burned. CO2 from burning is biogenic (excluded from Scope 1 GHG Protocol totals). GWP100 values: CH4=27.9, N2O=273 (IPCC AR6 WGI Table 7.SM.7).
emission_factors.notes: Punjab, Haryana, western UP burn ~23 million tonnes rice straw annually pre-harvest. India total rice straw burned: ~25–30 Mt/yr (ICAR estimates). Burning is illegal but widespread due to short transplanting window between kharif harvest and rabi sowing. CO (not counted in GHG total): ~102 g/kg DM burned (IPCC 2006 Table 2.5). Burning efficiency: ~88% of available straw actually combusted (IPCC 2006 Tier 1 assumption). This EF applies per tonne of dry matter (DM) actually burned, not per tonne available.

emission_factors.factor_name: Crop Residue Burning – Wheat Straw – India
emission_factors.source_document: IPCC 2006 Vol 4 Ch 2 (Table 2.5); MoEFCC Third National Communication 2023; ICAR
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC 2006 Vol 4 Ch 2 Tier 1 EFs for wheat straw burning (Table 2.5). CH4 EF: 2.7 g/kg DM burned = 2.7 kgCH4/t DM; CO2e = 2.7 × 27.9 = 75.33 kgCO2e/t DM. N2O EF: 0.07 g/kg DM burned = 0.07 kgN2O/t DM; CO2e = 0.07 × 273 = 19.11 kgCO2e/t DM. Total (CH4 + N2O): 94.4 kgCO2e/t DM burned. CO2 from burning is biogenic (excluded from Scope 1 GHG Protocol totals). GWP100 values: CH4=27.9, N2O=273 (IPCC AR6 WGI Table 7.SM.7).
emission_factors.notes: Wheat straw burning occurs mainly in Punjab and Haryana post-rabi harvest (April–May). Total wheat straw burned in India: ~15–20 Mt/yr. IPCC 2006 uses same Tier 1 EF for cereals (rice, wheat, barley, etc.) from Table 2.5. CO (not counted): ~102 g/kg DM burned. Burning fraction varies by region and mechanisation; combined harvester increases straw availability for burning. This EF is per tonne DM actually burned.

emission_factors.factor_name: Crop Residue Burning – Sugarcane Trash (Pre-Harvest Burning) – India
emission_factors.source_document: IPCC 2006 Vol 4 Ch 2 (Table 2.5); MoEFCC Third National Communication 2023; ICAR
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC 2006 Vol 4 Ch 2 Tier 1 EFs for sugarcane trash burning (Table 2.5). CH4 EF: 1.3 g/kg DM burned = 1.3 kgCH4/t DM; CO2e = 1.3 × 27.9 = 36.27 kgCO2e/t DM. N2O EF: 0.11 g/kg DM burned = 0.11 kgN2O/t DM; CO2e = 0.11 × 273 = 30.03 kgCO2e/t DM. Total (CH4 + N2O): 66.3 kgCO2e/t DM burned. CO2 from burning is biogenic (excluded from Scope 1 GHG Protocol totals). GWP100 values: CH4=27.9, N2O=273 (IPCC AR6 WGI Table 7.SM.7).
emission_factors.notes: Sugarcane trash burning is common in Maharashtra, Karnataka, and Tamil Nadu before manual harvesting to remove leaves. India sugarcane area ~5 million ha. IPCC 2006 Vol 4 Table 2.5 provides different EF for sugarcane vs cereal crops: lower CH4 (1.3 vs 2.7 g/kg) but higher N2O (0.11 vs 0.07 g/kg). CO (not counted): ~106 g/kg DM burned (IPCC Table 2.5 sugarcane value). This EF applies per tonne trash actually burned (not per tonne cane harvested).

emission_factors.source_document: IPCC 2006 Vol 2 Ch 4 (Fugitive Emissions); MoEFCC Third National Communication 2023; Pandey et al. (2022), Elementa: Science of the Anthropocene
emission_factors.methodology: India-specific CH4 emission factor for underground coal mining: 5.05 kg CH4/tonne coal (2018 field measurement data from Pandey et al. 2022, consistent with IPCC 2006 Vol 2 Ch 4 Tier 2 methodology). CO2e = 5.05 × GWP100(CH4) = 5.05 × 27.9 = 140.9 kgCO2e/t coal. IPCC 2006 Vol 2 Ch 4 Tier 1 uses m3 CH4/tonne coal; conversion: CH4 density at STP = 0.671 kg/m3. Includes only methane fugitive emissions; excludes CO2 from coal combustion or diesel/explosive use in mining operations.
emission_factors.notes: Methane released from coal seams during underground mining. India underground coal mines include Jharia (BCCL), Eastern Coalfields (ECL), and Central Coalfields Limited operations. Methane (CH4) is the dominant fugitive GHG. IPCC 2006 Vol 2 Ch 4 Tier 1 range for underground mines: 1–25 m3/tonne coal; India average ~7.5 m3/t (based on 5.05 kg/t ÷ 0.671 kg/m3). MoEFCC TNC 2023 uses India-specific mine-level data consistent with this range. Methane capture (CMM/VAM) can reduce this EF by 30-60% in newer BCCL operations.

emission_factors.source_document: IPCC 2006 Vol 2 Ch 4 (Fugitive Emissions); MoEFCC Third National Communication 2023; Pandey et al. (2022), Elementa: Science of the Anthropocene
emission_factors.methodology: India-specific CH4 emission factor for surface/opencast coal mining: 0.87 kg CH4/tonne coal (2018 field measurement data from Pandey et al. 2022, consistent with IPCC 2006 Vol 2 Ch 4 Tier 2 methodology). CO2e = 0.87 × GWP100(CH4) = 0.87 × 27.9 = 24.3 kgCO2e/t coal. IPCC 2006 Vol 2 Ch 4 Tier 1 uses m3 CH4/tonne coal; conversion: CH4 density at STP = 0.671 kg/m3. Includes only methane fugitive emissions; excludes CO2 from coal combustion or diesel/explosive use in mining operations.
emission_factors.notes: Methane released from overburden removal and coal face exposure in opencast mining. Surface mining dominates India coal production: ~92% of Coal India's output is opencast. Much lower CH4 EF than underground due to less confined seams and faster gas dispersion. IPCC 2006 Vol 2 Ch 4 Tier 1 range for surface mines: 0.3–2 m3/tonne. India surface mine average ~1.3 m3/t (0.87 kg/t ÷ 0.671 kg/m3). Coal India Limited (CIL) operates the world's largest coal mining network; major opencast projects include Gevra (Chhattisgarh), Jayant (MP), Dudhichua (UP).

emission_factors.factor_name: Paper and Pulp – Process CO2 from Lime Kiln Calcination (Kraft Process)
emission_factors.source_document: IPCC 2006 Vol 3 Ch 2 (Lime Production, Section 2.3); GHG Protocol Pulp and Paper Guidance
emission_factors.methodology: Process CO2 from carbonate decomposition (CaCO3 → CaO + CO2) in the lime kiln of the kraft chemical pulping process. Makeup lime consumption in kraft cycle: ~0.10–0.15 t CaCO3/t pulp (net consumption after lime recovery and recausticising efficiency losses). CO2 per t CaCO3 calcined = 44/100 = 0.44 tCO2. Process CO2 per t pulp: 0.10–0.15 × 0.44 × 1000 = 44–66 kgCO2/t pulp. Per tonne paper (typically ~0.9 t chemical pulp/t paper, plus recycled fibre): applied GHG Protocol tool guidance value of ~100 kgCO2/t paper, consistent with integrated kraft mill data for India (IPMA members). This is process CO2 from inorganic carbonate decomposition per IPCC Vol 3 scope; excludes fossil fuel combustion in the lime kiln.
emission_factors.notes: Applies to kraft (chemical) pulp and paper process only. Mechanical pulp (CTMP, groundwood) has essentially zero process CO2 from lime. India's major kraft mills include ITC Bhadrachalam, JK Paper, Seshasayee Paper (Tamil Nadu), and West Coast Paper Mills. Process CO2 is a small fraction of total mill CO2: energy-related combustion (recovery boiler, power boiler) typically contributes 800–1500 kgCO2/t paper. Black liquor combustion CO2 is biogenic (not counted in Scope 1).

emission_factors.factor_name: Ammonia Production – Steam Methane Reforming (Natural Gas) – Process CO2
emission_factors.source_document: IPCC 2006 Vol 3 Ch 3 Chemical Industry (Table 3.1)
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC 2006 Vol 3 Ch 3 Tier 1 default: Table 3.1 emission factor for natural gas SMR (steam methane reforming, full oxidation process): 1.694 tCO2/tonne NH3 = 1694 kgCO2/t NH3. This covers process CO2 from steam reforming: CH4 + H2O → CO + 3H2; CO + H2O → CO2 + H2. Total fuel requirement: 30.2 GJ(NCV)/tonne NH3; carbon content of natural gas → CO2. For partial oxidation (residual fuel oil): default is different (Table 3.1). India almost entirely uses natural gas SMR for ammonia (Rashtriya Chemicals, GNFC, IFFCO, KRIBHCO, NFL, Chambal Fertilisers). CO2 that is captured for urea production (0.733 tCO2/tonne urea) should be subtracted: net emission = 1.694 − (urea production fraction × 0.733) kgCO2/t NH3.
emission_factors.notes: India is the 3rd largest ammonia producer globally (mostly for fertilisers). ~80% of India's ammonia goes to urea production (FICC data). For integrated ammonia-urea plants: net CO2 from ammonia = 1694 − 0.733×1000×(NH3-to-urea ratio) = 1694 − 733 × 1.18 (stoichiometric ratio) ≈ 1694 − 865 ≈ 829 kgCO2/t NH3 (net after urea CO2 use). Standalone ammonia without urea integration: 1694 kgCO2/t NH3. Green ammonia (electrolysis H2 + Haber-Bosch): near-zero process CO2.

emission_factors.factor_name: Nitric Acid Production – N2O Process Emissions (India Average)
emission_factors.source_document: IPCC 2006 Vol 3 Ch 3 Chemical Industry (Table 3.3)
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC 2006 Vol 3 Ch 3 Tier 1: N2O emission factor from nitric acid production (Table 3.3). N2O is generated during catalytic oxidation of ammonia (NH3 + O2 → NO + N2O). India average EF: 6.0 kgN2O/tonne HNO3 (medium-pressure plants without abatement). IPCC Table 3.3 range: 2 kgN2O/t (plants with NSCR abatement) to 9 kgN2O/t (high-pressure unabated). CO2e = 6.0 × GWP100(N2O=273) = 1638 kgCO2e/t HNO3. India uses predominantly medium-pressure Ostwald process; most older plants lack N2O abatement. IPCC guidance: use highest EF (9 kg/t) when technology is unknown.
emission_factors.notes: India nitric acid production: ~4.5 Mt/yr (fertiliser-grade, 98% concentration). Major producers: GNFC Bharuch, NFL, Chambal Fertilisers. N2O factor 6.0 kgN2O/t represents India mix of medium-pressure plants without abatement = 1638 kgCO2e/t HNO3 (AR6 GWP). With NSCR abatement: 2 kgN2O/t = 546 kgCO2e/t HNO3. With extended absorption + NSCR: ~0.5 kgN2O/t = 137 kgCO2e/t HNO3 (best practice). N2O from nitric acid is one of the highest-GWP industrial process emissions per unit value.

emission_factors.factor_name: Industrial Wastewater – Aerobic Treatment (Activated Sludge) – Process GHG Emissions
emission_factors.source_document: IPCC 2006 Vol 5 Ch 6 (Wastewater Treatment and Discharge)
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC 2006 Vol 5 Ch 6 Tier 1 methodology for industrial wastewater. CH4 component: MCF (aerobic) ≈ 0.001 (fugitive from sludge settling/digestion); Bo = 0.25 kgCH4/kgCOD; COD = 2 kgCOD/m3 (Tier 1 default for mixed industrial WW); CH4 = 0.001 × 0.25 × 2 = 0.0005 kgCH4/m3 → 0.014 kgCO2e/m3. N2O component from biological nitrogen removal (nitrification/denitrification): N2O EF ≈ 0.005 kgN2O/kgN removed (IPCC 2006 default for aerobic WW treatment); at N loading 50 mgN/L (0.05 kgN/m3): N2O = 0.003 kgN2O/m3 × (44/28) × 273 = 0.104 kgCO2e/m3. Total (CH4 + N2O): 0.014 + 0.104 ≈ 0.12 kgCO2e/m3.
emission_factors.notes: Applies to aerobic effluent treatment plants (ETP) in industrial facilities (textiles, pharmaceuticals, food processing, etc.). COD of actual effluent varies widely (0.5–20 kgCOD/m3 depending on industry sector). For high-strength industrial WW (food/beverage, 5 kgCOD/m3): aerobic CH4 is still low but energy for aeration increases significantly. N2O is the dominant GHG in aerobic systems; highly variable with N loading and process control. This factor has high uncertainty (±50%); prefer site-specific measurements for reporting.

emission_factors.factor_name: Industrial Wastewater – Anaerobic Treatment (Without CH4 Recovery) – Process GHG Emissions
emission_factors.source_document: IPCC 2006 Vol 5 Ch 6 (Wastewater Treatment and Discharge)
emission_factors.methodology: IPCC 2006 Vol 5 Ch 6 Tier 1: CH4 from anaerobic industrial wastewater treatment. Equation 6.3: CH4_i = (TOW_i × EF_i) − R where EF_i = Bo_i × MCF_i. Bo (maximum CH4 production) = 0.25 kgCH4/kgCOD (IPCC 2006 Table 6.2). MCF (methane correction factor, anaerobic without CH4 recovery) = 0.8 (IPCC Table 6.4). COD concentration = 2.0 kgCOD/m3 (Tier 1 default for mixed industrial WW). CH4 per m3 = 0.25 × 0.8 × 2.0 = 0.400 kgCH4/m3. CO2e = 0.400 × 27.9 = 11.16 kgCO2e/m3. N2O negligible in purely anaerobic conditions. CH4 recovery = 0 assumed (vented or flared without energy recovery).
emission_factors.notes: Anaerobic treatment (UASB reactors, anaerobic lagoons) common in India for high-strength industrial effluents: sugar mills, distilleries, paper mills, tanneries. If CH4 is captured and combusted for energy: subtract recovered CH4 from this factor. With biogas recovery (common in sugar/distillery sector): actual emission can be near zero if CH4 captured and used for heat/power. COD varies significantly: distillery spent wash ~80 kgCOD/m3, textile effluent ~1 kgCOD/m3, pharma effluent ~5 kgCOD/m3. Scale linearly with actual COD: EF = actual COD × 0.25 × 0.8 × 27.9 kgCO2e/m3.
